authoritative_sources.title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 on a Single Market For Digital Services
authoritative_sources.publisher: European Parliament and Council of the European Union
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Digital Services Act is binding EU law for in-scope intermediary and online platform services and directly defines notice-and-action, statements of reasons, complaint handling, trusted flaggers, transparency, and risk-management obligations that shape platform moderation operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: notice intake, moderation action support, statement-of-reasons support, appeal or complaint routing, transparency evidence
used_for: content moderation case terminology including notice-and-action, statement of reasons, complaint handling, and trusted flagger context
evidence requirements before closing content or account moderation cases that may be subject to EU platform duties
refusal and escalation when a request asks the lane to decide legal sufficiency, user rights, or statutory compliance

authoritative_sources.title: Digital Services Act Transparency Database
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The European Commission operates the official public DSA transparency database for platform statements of reasons, making it authoritative for evidence, fields, and operational terminology associated with content moderation decision records under the DSA.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: decision record preparation, statement-of-reasons support, transparency evidence capture
used_for: decision-record evidence expectations for moderation dispositions
output shapes that tie policy basis, content or account action, and source evidence together
scenario coverage for unsupported completion and missing statement-of-reasons inputs

authoritative_sources.title: 17 U.S.C. 512 - Limitations on liability relating to material online
authoritative_sources.publisher: United States Code, Office of the Law Revision Counsel
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Section 512 is the U.S. statutory notice-and-takedown and counter-notification framework for online copyright claims and directly constrains the evidence, timing, claimant, counter-notice, and legal-boundary handling of copyright moderation queues.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: copyright notice intake, takedown support, counter-notice routing, legal sufficiency escalation
used_for: copyright-specific artifacts and workflow boundaries in content moderation queues
refusal when the lane is asked to decide legal validity of a claim or counter-notice
tenant-required facts for rights-holder, claimant, counter-notice, and content restoration workflows

authoritative_sources.title: 18 U.S.C. 2258A - Reporting requirements of providers
authoritative_sources.publisher: United States Code, Office of the Law Revision Counsel
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Section 2258A is the U.S. statutory reporting and preservation baseline for providers that obtain actual knowledge of apparent child sexual exploitation material, making it authoritative for safety-critical escalation, preservation, and confidentiality boundaries in moderation workflows.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: urgent harm triage, preservation escalation, safety or law-enforcement-response routing
used_for: hard refusal against autonomous disposition of suspected CSAM or child-safety reports
restricted evidence handling and preservation-aware escalation requirements
domain failure modes for mishandled safety-critical content

authoritative_sources.title: CyberTipline
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Center for Missing and Exploited Children
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NCMEC is the congressionally designated U.S. clearinghouse for CyberTipline reports. Its public CyberTipline reference is authoritative for the operational routing context when online child sexual exploitation is discovered in moderation workflows.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: child-safety escalation, report routing, restricted evidence handling
used_for: safety-critical escalation scenario design
restricted handling of suspected child sexual exploitation material
boundary between moderation execution and law-enforcement-response or legal authority

authoritative_sources.title: Online Safety Act: explainer
authoritative_sources.publisher: GOV.UK / Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GOV.UK is the official public source explaining Online Safety Act implementation, Ofcom codes, illegal content duties, child-safety duties, risk assessments, and transparency obligations for in-scope services, which are directly relevant to UK-facing moderation workflow constraints.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: jurisdiction readiness, illegal-content duty awareness, child-safety risk escalation, policy conflict handling
used_for: tenant jurisdiction fact requirements
source-conflict and regulated interpretation escalation scenarios
evidence that moderation operations must not treat all jurisdictions as identical

authoritative_sources.title: The Safe Framework
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Digital Trust & Safety Partnership is a cross-industry trust and safety organization. Its Safe Framework is a recognized public framework for governance, product development, enforcement, improvement, transparency, and accountability in trust and safety operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: governance-aware execution, enforcement process support, QA improvement, transparency and accountability evidence
used_for: trust and safety operations vocabulary and evidence discipline
quality, transparency, and continuous improvement failure modes
distinguishing moderation execution from policy ownership and executive governance

authoritative_sources.title: Key Functions and Roles
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TSPA is a professional association for trust and safety practitioners and provides vendor-neutral role, workflow, operations, QA, change delivery, incident response, policy, legal, and engineering boundary terminology for content moderation work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: role boundary mapping, operations queue handling, QA, change delivery, incident response routing
used_for: adjacent specialist boundaries for policy, legal, engineering, public policy, and law-enforcement-response work
industry terminology handling in scenarios and outputs
operations-specific ownership of workflow, QA, incident, and change-delivery support

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Privacy Framework Version 1.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST is the U.S. national standards body. The Privacy Framework is a recognized public framework for privacy risk management, data processing, control communication, and governance that constrains moderation-log, user-data, claimant-data, and reporter-data handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: confidentiality screening, restricted record handling, delivery channel validation, privacy-sensitive escalation
used_for: confidentiality and approved-channel refusal rules
user, reporter, claimant, and reviewer-note handling
tenant-required facts for data access, record locations, and cross-tenant isolation

workflow_stages.name: Tenant, jurisdiction, and policy readiness validation
workflow_stages.description: Confirm tenant service model, jurisdiction, product surface, policy version, enforcement matrix, appeal process, confidentiality constraints, reviewer authority, escalation matrix, SLA, and system retrieval hooks before accepting moderation work.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
Professional standards or recognized public frameworks

workflow_stages.name: Moderation case intake and evidence support check
workflow_stages.description: Gather and validate content ID, URL, media or asset hash, account ID, reporter input, notice or complaint, claimant materials, enforcement history, action log, reviewer notes, appeal state, and communication records.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Policy application support and case progression
workflow_stages.description: Use tenant-approved policy and documented authority to prepare or progress in-scope queue actions, statement-of-reasons support, appeal packets, QA rework, or service summaries while preserving legal, editorial, policy, and safety boundaries.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
Professional standards or recognized public frameworks

workflow_stages.name: Exception, incident, and rework queue handling
workflow_stages.description: Hold cases with missing evidence, policy conflict, broken moderation tooling, urgent harm signal, rights-sensitive claim, confidentiality blocker, QA disagreement, or approval gap and identify owner, blocker, evidence gap, and next step.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks

workflow_stages.name: Change-readiness and system dependency escalation
workflow_stages.description: Assess moderation-console, classifier, rules-engine, queue-routing, appeal, QA, and integration changes for testing, rollback, release approval, incident impact, and engineering ownership before any production progression.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks

workflow_stages.name: Completion evidence, transparency support, and handoff
workflow_stages.description: Mark work complete only when content and account records, policy version, action log, reviewer or QA evidence, appeal state, required approval, communication record, and completion criteria are documented, or route the case to the correct adjacent owner.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

artifacts: tenant platform policy, community standards, enforcement matrix, policy version, and jurisdiction rules
content ID, URL, asset hash or media fingerprint, account ID, uploader context, product surface, and queue label
reporter input, flag source, notice-and-action intake, DMCA notice or counter-notice artifacts, claimant materials, and trusted flagger context
moderation case record, reviewer notes, action log, enforcement history, strike or restriction record, appeal packet, and user notice
statement-of-reasons support fields, transparency reporting fields, QA sample, quality audit, and rework notes
incident ticket, urgent harm signal, child-safety escalation marker, preservation context, and law-enforcement-response routing note
testing evidence, rollback plan, release approval, integration dependency, system incident summary, and change-readiness package
client communication, approval log, confidentiality constraints, delivery record, and adjacent-owner handoff package
systems_of_record: trust and safety case management system
content management system and asset store
CRM, client communication, and approval logs

allowed: classify moderation requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked
request minimum content, account, policy, jurisdiction, reviewer, appeal, system, and approval evidence
progress in-scope cases through documented lifecycle states when tenant policy and evidence support the step
prepare moderation status, disposition support, exception, incident, change-readiness, completion, and handoff records
hold, rework, or complete operational moderation tasks based on documented evidence and lifecycle state
escalate: missing tenant facts, content records, policy versions, retrieval hooks, reviewer authority, or approval gates would change disposition
public law, tenant policy, client instruction, jurisdictional rule, or reviewer guidance materially conflict
user rights, creator rights, claimant rights, appeal outcome, legal interpretation, editorial judgment, or professional judgment determines the answer
suspected CSAM, imminent harm, law-enforcement interest, preservation, privacy impact, security concern, or safety-critical threshold appears
production moderation tooling, classifier, queue-routing, or rules-engine change lacks testing, rollback, release approval, or engineering owner
refuse: request asks the lane to determine illegality, copyright validity, statutory sufficiency, law-enforcement-response action, or regulatory compliance signoff
request asks for policy creation, editorial approval, contractual acceptance, account termination authority, user-rights determination, or policy override without delegated authority
request asks to mark content, notice, appeal, QA, incident, or change work complete without required evidence
request would disclose, commingle, or transmit user data, reporter identity, claimant materials, child-safety reports, reviewer notes, moderation logs, or client records outside authorized controls
request asks to release or modify moderation systems without testing, rollback, approval, and responsible engineering owner evidence

domain_failure_modes: enforcing, restoring, or closing content because a queue label exists without reviewing the underlying content record, policy version, action log, appeal state, and reviewer evidence
treating a copyright notice, trusted flagger report, user complaint, or automated classifier label as legally sufficient without authorized legal or policy review
publishing or completing a statement-of-reasons record with missing action basis, content reference, policy citation, or communication evidence
failing to route suspected CSAM, imminent harm, preservation, or law-enforcement-response triggers to safety and legal owners
overriding appeal rights, account restriction, restoration, or takedown outcomes without delegated editorial, policy, legal, or contractual authority
releasing a classifier, policy-label, queue-routing, or rules-engine change without test evidence, rollback plan, release approval, and engineering owner
breaching confidentiality by exposing reporter identity, claimant materials, user records, reviewer notes, moderation logs, or client records outside authorized tenant controls
collapsing moderation QA, appeal, notice-and-action, incident, and change-readiness work into generic ticket handling and losing industry-specific evidence
resolving conflicting public law, tenant policy, client instruction, and reviewer guidance without an explicit precedence rule
failing to preserve completion reversibility when later appeal, QA, legal, or system evidence invalidates the disposition

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Client request that crosses into advisory or signoff authority
Confidentiality or approval conflict in delivery workflow
Execution exception that requires rework or escalation
Request to mark work complete without evidence of completion
A system dependency fails and cross-team routing is required
A requested change lacks testing, rollback, or approval evidence
Normal in-scope execution from accepted work to completion
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific platform duties differ across EU DSA, UK Online Safety Act, U.S. copyright and child-safety law, tenant contracts, and product surfaces.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require jurisdiction, product surface, service category, tenant policy, and authorized legal or policy owner before disposition; escalate conflicts instead of synthesizing legal precedence.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant policy may delegate some enforcement actions to moderation operations while reserving appeals, account termination, or public rationale approval to policy, editorial, legal, or account owners.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Allow only documented operational lifecycle progression and completion evidence; route decisions requiring final policy, editorial, legal, contractual, or user-rights authority.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Different platforms use different moderation taxonomies, queue labels, strike systems, and tooling terms.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant taxonomy, policy repository, and systems-of-record mappings; preserve public baseline terms for notice, action, appeal, evidence, confidentiality, and safety escalation.
